title: How Accurately Can We Predict the Melting Points of Drug-like Compounds?
enumeration_method: Full read of article_text.txt + NXML inspection of all 7 tables + grep for common compound names (aspirin/caffeine/benzene/etc.) + grep for '°C' and 'melting point' contexts
notes: This paper develops a consensus QSPR model for predicting MPs of drug-like compounds.
It uses four large data sets (Bergström n=277, Bradley n=3041, OCHEM n=21883, Enamine n=22404) totaling >47k compounds.
The paper itself contains NO per-compound MP values with named compounds. All 7 tables report aggregate statistics:
- Table 1: RMSE of OCHEM-trained models across test sets (RMSE numbers only)
- Table 2: RMSE of Enamine-trained models across test sets (RMSE numbers only)
- Table 3: Counts of outlying compounds at various p-value thresholds
- Table 4: Examples of functional/reactive groups overrepresented in outliers (graphic; group names like 'carboxylic acid', not compounds)
- Table 5: Estimated vs calculated RMSE per data set
- Table 6: Consensus model RMSEs across train/test combinations
- Table 7: Cross-validated RMSE of final model split by drug-like/outside interval
Mentions of specific temperatures (e.g. '17 °C', '150 °C', '50–250 °C', '<0 °C') are bounds, distribution descriptors, or RMSE numbers — never a MP measurement of a named compound.
All actual MP data for the 47k+ compounds is hosted externally at http://ochem.eu/article/55638 — not in this article or its NXML/text.
No common drug-like compounds (aspirin, caffeine, paracetamol, etc.) are mentioned by name anywhere in the article body.
